title-group.article-title: An adolescent female African elephant (Loxodonta africana) gave birth to an abnormal calf

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceYujing Duan, Beijing Wildlife Park, Beijing 100000, P. R. China. Email: 895583178@qq.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2023 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Maternal nutrition and the uterine environment can influence placental development in mammals, leading to the birth of abnormal infants who often experience difficulties in independent standing. This article documents an adolescent female African elephant (Loxodonta africana) giving birth to an African elephant calf with a shoulder height below the mean, and its inability to stand for the first 10 h after birth, a time span that significantly exceeds the average duration for independent standing. Through the implementation of assisted feeding and assisted standing measures, the calf eventually achieved independent standing and nursing from its mother after 27 h and subsequent catch‐up growth at 5 months of age. This study presents the first case report on the growth parameters during pregnancy of an adolescent African elephant, the birth and care (feeding, assisted standing) of a small African elephant calf with the inability to stand alone and nurse independently for several hours, highlighting pregnancy may influence the growth of cows during their ongoing growth and development phase, the calf's survival and subsequent catch‐up growth at 5 months of age. These findings offer valuable insights into the care of abnormal African elephant calves.

This study documents the birth of an African elephant calf with a shoulder height below the mean, which displayed the inability to stand independently for the initial 10 h post‐partum. With artificial care, the calf achieved independent standing and suckling from the cow, as well as catch‐up growth at 5 months.

p.mixed-citation: Duan, Y., Wang, Z., Li, J., Zhang, M., & Bi, Z. (2024). An adolescent female African elephant (Loxodonta africana) gave birth to an abnormal calf. Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1331. 10.1002/vms3.1331PMC1076603837990940

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: The African elephant (Loxodonta africana) is the largest land mammal, with various factors influencing its growth and reproductive processes. Adolescence typically spans from 5 to 10 years in elephants, with puberty at 8 to 12 years. The gestation period ranges from ∼20 to 22 months. Breeding is rare before the age of 10 years in the wild (European Association of Zoos and Aquaria, 2020); the average age of the first reproduction for female African elephants in the wild is 13.8 years (Lee et al., 2016). Under human care, elephants can reproduce earlier than average, with records of the earliest first birth occurring in a female African elephant that conceived at 6 years and gave birth at 8 years (European Association of Zoos and Aquaria, 2020). However, there is no research on whether pregnancy affects the growth of female elephants in captive currently.

Furthermore, research studies explored the weight, shoulder height, pregnancy duration (Dale, 2010), and development milestones on the first day after birth for African elephant calves (Bercovitch & Andrews, 2010), and comprehensive information on artificial care for newborn abnormal African elephant calves and growth data for calves older than 1 month remains unavailable.

This article records and analyzes the growth of pregnant elephants during adolescence, as well as the artificial care and growth of the abnormal calf. The findings may offer valuable guidance for the breeding and reproductive management of African elephants in zoos.

sec.title: STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
sec.p: Each mean score is accompanied by the standard error of the mean (±SEM). The impact of pregnancy, sex, and age on the shoulder height increase of African elephants was evaluated using one‐way analysis of variance. All analyses were conducted utilizing the SPSS 23.0 software, with statistical significance set at p values less than 0.05.

sec.title: Cow
sec.p: The cow, named ‘Yu huan', was introduced in 2016 and estimated to be 3–4 years old at the time, with a shoulder height of 160 cm. In June 2021, the shoulder height measured 210 cm, increasing to 220 cm in June 2022, and further to 225 cm in June 2023 (Table 1), indicating a growth of 15 cm during pregnancy. During the observed period, ‘He qi' and ‘Ru yi' displayed a height increase of less than 15 cm in pregnancy, while ‘Mei lun', who miscarried in 2022, exhibited a similar growth pattern. Contrarily, ‘Yu rao' and ‘Zhi xiang', without any history of pregnancy, demonstrated a height increase of 24 cm, and ‘Mei huan' exhibited a height increase of 11 cm (Table 1). Analysis presented in Table 2 suggests that pregnancy moderately decreased the shoulder height increase in female elephants (p = 0.078), although this finding was not statistically significant. Conversely, both sex (Table 3) and age (Table 4) were found to have no significant effect on the shoulder height increase (p > 0.05) of African elephants.

caption.p: Elephants' shoulder height increase (cm) from June 2021 to June 2023.
Effect of pregnancy on shoulder height increase of female African elephants.

fn.p: Note: The number of animals contributing to each mean is shown in parentheses (N). Each mean score is accompanied by the standard error of the mean (±SEM).

caption.p: Effect of sex on shoulder height increase of African elephants.

fn.p: Note: The number of animals contributing to each mean is shown in parentheses (N). Each mean score is accompanied by the standard error of the mean (±SEM).

caption.p: Effect of age on shoulder height increase of African elephants.

fn.p: Note: The number of animals contributing to each mean is shown in parentheses (N). Each mean score is accompanied by the standard error of the mean (±SEM). a presents p‐value between age 9 and 10. b presents p‐value between age 10 and 11. c presents p‐value between age 9 and 11.

sec.p: Based on the ‘Body Condition Score' guidelines for elephants (Morfeld et al., 2014), ‘Yu huan' consistently scored six points from 2021 to 2023 (Table 5), indicating an optimal physical condition during the pregnancy and early lactation. There were no medical issues reported during the management of ‘Yu huan’. Throughout her pregnancy, she had access to food, including hay, and received daily supplementation of calcium, vitamins, and minerals. Despite ample forage, her food intake, especially hay and pellets, did not increase during the later stages of pregnancy (Table 6). ‘Yu huan' ranked fifth among a population of 12 African elephants and was fourth highest‐ranking female.

The cow mated on 12 April 12021, at 01:26 PM, with the mating lasting for 1 min. On 25 February 2023, at 2:00 AM, the elephant exhibited restlessness and agitation, and at dawn at 5:10 AM, she gave birth to a female calf. The gestation period lasted 684 days. After birth, the cow attempted to help the calf stand using her front legs, but her efforts were unsuccessful. With human assistance the next day, both the cow and calf successfully initiated nursing.

sec.title: Calf
sec.p: The calf is a fully developed female with bright eyes and vocalizations. Its skin is dark brown. The raspy thoracic noises heard at birth, indicative of fluid in the respiratory tract, disappeared as the calf began to walk. The umbilical cord, remaining attached to the calf, measures 15 cm in length and 1 cm in width. It was disinfected daily and detached on the 20th day. The weight was estimated as 85 kg by the keeper. After birth, the calf initially attempted to stand with the help of the cow but failed. Consequently, artificial feeding and assisted standing procedures begin 10 h after birth. On the 27th hour, the calf achieved successful independent standing and initiated nursing from its mother.

The calf's measurements at birth were as follows: shoulder height 80 cm, body length 85 cm, and tail length 35 cm. At 1 month of age, the calf measured 83 cm in shoulder height, 90 cm in body length, and 38 cm in tail length. At 2 months old, the shoulder height increased to 92 cm, body length to 105 cm, and tail length to 40 cm. The calf's measurements at 3 months were a shoulder height of 95 cm, body length of 110 cm, and tail length of 43 cm. At 4 months old, the shoulder height measured 97 cm, body length 120 cm, and tail length 45 cm. At 5 months, the shoulder height reached 108 cm, while the body length and tail length remained consistent at 120 and 45 cm, respectively (Table 7).

caption.p: Calf's shoulder height、body length、and tail length (cm).

sec.title: Enclosure cleaning after parturition
sec.p: Approximately 2 h after parturition, the blood and amniotic fluid mixture on the cement floor of the maternity room were cleaned. Clean bedding grass was placed, ensuring regular ventilation and maintaining an indoor temperature of 20–25°C.

sec.title: Assisted feeding
sec.p: Due to the calf's inability to stand and nurse from the mother, assisted feeding was introduced. At 10 and 12 h post‐birth, the calf received 200 mL of formula milk with a concentration of 14.2% (prepared at a ratio of 1:6). Subsequently, at 15 and 25 h post‐birth, the calf was supplemented with 300 mL of formula milk with the same concentration. After the initial three milk supplements, a 10% glucose solution was administered by the feeder. The formula milk used for artificial feeding (Mead Johnson, Enfagrow, Stage 2) was diluted with purified water at 40°C at a ratio of 1:6. The glucose solution was provided for fluid supplementation (Table 8).

sec.title: Assisted standing
sec.p: Since the calf was unable to stand unaided after birth, assisted standing was implemented. The cow and calf were temporarily separated into adjacent enclosures, allowing the cow's trunk to interact with the calf through the bars. Two assisted standing methods were employed: (1) using a sheet, two caretakers lifted the calf's abdomen from both sides, facilitating standing on all four legs; (2) recognizing the calf's struggle to lift its hind legs, caretakers supported the calf's front legs with their feet and guided its step‐by‐step attempts to stand. Assistance was provided at intervals of 10, 13, 14, and 27 h after birth, with the final 40‐min session marking the calf's successful independent standing (Table 9).

sec.title: Post‐partum care for the cow
sec.p: The cow received free access to clean water and a diet that included 20 kg of banana, 2.5 kg of sugar cane, 7.5 kg of carrots, 7.5 kg of pellets (containing minerals and vitamins), 500 g of brown sugar, 100 g of milk powder, and free‐choice hay. Additional supplements included herba leonuri preparations, calcium tablets, vitamins, and minerals. The feed was adjusted to accommodate appetite recovery and changing nutritional requirements.

sec.title: Possible reasons for abnormal
sec.p: According to the findings of Dale (2010), the average shoulder height and weight of female African elephant calves were reported as 86.8 ± 1.1 cm and 95.1 ± 2.5 kg, respectively. In this case, the calf's measured shoulder height at birth was 80 cm, below the reported average. Moreover, the calf's delayed ability to stand independently for more than 10 h post‐birth surpassed the expected norm, signifying its abnormal condition.

Fetal growth is influenced by maternal factors (such as maternal health and nutritional status), fetal factors (genetic composition), and placental function (Maulik, 2006). The gestation period for African elephants is reported to be 645.2 ± 3.0 days (Dale, 2010), while the calf in this study exhibited a full‐term pregnancy lasting 684 days. The timing and duration of delivery were consistent with recorded African elephant births (Kowalski et al., 2010), effectively ruling out premature birth and difficult delivery as causes.

The uterine environment can modify genetic factors, and the size of the elephant calf is related to the size of the mother elephant. Smaller‐sized mother elephants tend to give birth to smaller‐sized calves (Laws, 1996). The cow in this study was small in stature, and the calf positioning between her front legs (Laws, 1996) enabled to reach the nipple to feeding. This behaviour is consistent with elephants walking under their mothers during much of their first year of life (Buechner et al., 1963), indicating that the calf's small size is attributed to its mother's size. Although growth data for elephants within their first year are lacking, it can be speculated that the calf exhibited caught‐up comparable to the average shoulder height increase from birth to 1 year of age (Laws, 1996) within its initial 5 months.

The calf's prolonged inability to stand independently after birth suggested fetal development issues. Placental insufficiency can hinder fetal adequate growth and development throughout pregnancy, stemming from an inability to fulfil the fetus's nutritional requirements (Damhuis et al., 2021). Primiparous mothers possess a heightened risk of placental formation disorders (Michaeli et al., 2022), and the cow in this study was indeed primiparous. Nevertheless, the lack of available maternal blood tests, urine tests, and ultrasound data during pregnancy complicates the determination of any specific placental function problems.

Restricted maternal nutrition in sheep has demonstrated the capacity to influence placental development, nutrient transport, lipid metabolism, and subsequent implications for neonatal survival and long‐term health (Steinhauser et al., 2021). Malnourished older elephants may struggle to sustain full‐term pregnancies or give birth to a healthy fetus, potentially failing to meet the nutritional needs of their calves (Mar et al., 2012). In the current study, the cow conceived at 8 years old and gave birth at 10 years old, a period encompassing ongoing growth and development (European Association of Zoos and Aquaria, 2020). Over the 684‐day gestation period, the cow's shoulder height increased by 15 cm. The current report indicates that neither the sex nor the age of African elephants had an impact on shoulder height increase. However, it is suggested that pregnancy moderately affects the shoulder height increase in female elephants. Hence, it is plausible that pregnancy may influence the growth of cows during their ongoing growth and development phase. Expanding the sample size and implementing continuous monitoring could provide a more accurate understanding of the impact of pregnancy on the shoulder height increase of adolescent female African elephants. Despite receiving ample forage daily, the cow did not increase her food intake during the later stages of pregnancy. Consistent body condition scores indicated her ideal physical state. Thus, the cow's nutrition seemed not to affect her body type or ability to sustain the pregnancy. However, without comprehensive testing data, it is challenging to ascertain if the fetus received sufficient nutrition for its development.

sec.title: The importance of early artificial care for the calves
sec.p: Calve standing times post‐birth for African elephant calves usually range from 0.5 to 90 min, with walking within the first hour and nursing within 4.5 h (Bercovitch & Andrews, 2010). According to Kowalski, African elephant calves should be capable of independent standing within 5–90 min (Kowalski et al., 2005). In contrast, the calf in this study required 27 h to stand independently, representing a duration 6–10 times longer than the norm. Calves unable to stand within 24 h of birth typically face a bleak prognosis. Records highlight an Asian elephant calf, ‘L', requiring 6 h to walk after birth and not surviving beyond its first year (Wiedner et al., 2017). Nonetheless, instances of Asian elephants overcoming birth difficulties through artificial care are also documented. For instance, elephant calf ‘C' faced challenges in standing, suckling, a torn umbilicus, respiratory arrest, arrhythmia, and a stature too short for nursing. With medical intervention and assisted care, the calf's standing issues were resolved within 24 h, enabling its survival beyond a year (Wiedner et al., 2017).

Blood calcium (Wichman et al., 2022) and glucose (Larson‐Peine et al., 2022) levels influence neonatal standing times post‐birth. Neonates encounter difficulties in standing due to reduced muscle tension (Michaeli et al., 2022), yet with artificial energy supplementation and assisted training, they can regain strength. For the calf in this study, both assisted feeding and standing interventions were employed. In the first assisted standing method, the calf could not sustain its standing after the sheet's removal, leading to a prone position. In the second method, the calf often fell to one side during attempts to stand, pointing to weakness in the forelimb muscles rather than the ground's slipperiness as the root cause. Although African elephant calves usually commence nursing 6–21 h after birth, and their demand for glucose is not urgent (Bercovitch & Andrews, 2010), the calf in this study's prolonged exertion and absence of suckling prompted the provision of formula milk to ensure necessary nutrition, supplemented with glucose for rapid energy restoration. The calf regained strength after multiple supplements. Initial assisted standing sessions proved ineffective, lasting less than 10 min each. However, after a night of rest and adjustment, a subsequent 40‐min assisted standing session led to substantial improvement in balance and muscle strength, underscoring the importance of prolonged assisted standing. This underscores the significance of early intervention and assistance in improving calf survival rates when addressing standing difficulties.

The calf's early artificial care played a pivotal role in addressing its standing and nursing issues after birth, consequently enhancing its survival prospects. Throughout assisted standing training, the cow and the calf were separated, capitalizing on the positive human–elephant relationship cultivated through daily care. To enhance calf care, it is advisable to implement positive reinforcement training in daily feeding management.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Yujing Duan was a major contributor in verifying and visualizing all the data and writing the manuscript. Zhencun Wang supervised and analyzed the work. Jiangtao Li guided the therapeutic regimen. Meng Zhang and Zhenchao Bi recorded data and administrated this project. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: ACKNOWLEGEMENTS
sec.p: The authors expressed thanks to the veterinary staff and keepers of the Beijing Wildlife Park for their participation in this work. Their input has greatly contributed to the quality and rigour of this study.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: None.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors confirm that the ethical policies of the journal, as noted on the journal's author guidelines page, have been adhered to. Zoo management granted permission for the research. The study adheres to all applicable Chinese laws.

sec.title: PEER REVIEW
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.1331.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data involving in the manuscript can be obtained from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
